audit_status.source_base: Hebrew 1 Samuel chapter 19, first-pass restored draft
audit_status.review_note: Reviewed for murder conspiracy, Jonathan's intervention, broken royal oath, repeated spear attack, Michal's warning and escape aid, household image, coercive cover story, sanctuary at Ramah, prophetic companies, disrobing, and mental-health misuse.

verses.restored: Saul spoke to Jonathan his son and to all his servants about putting David to death. Jonathan son of Saul delighted greatly in David.
Jonathan told David, saying, Saul my father seeks to put you to death. Now guard yourself in morning; stay in hidden place and conceal yourself.
I will go out and stand beside my father in field where you are, and I will speak about you to my father. If I see anything, I will tell you.
Jonathan spoke good of David to Saul his father and said, Let not king sin against his servant David, because he has not sinned against you and because his deeds toward you have been very good.

He put his life in his palm and struck Philistine, and YHWH made great deliverance for all Israel. You saw and rejoiced. Why then would you sin against innocent blood by putting David to death without cause?
Saul listened to voice of Jonathan, and Saul swore, As YHWH lives, he shall not be put to death.
Jonathan called David and told him all these words. Jonathan brought David to Saul, and he was before him as formerly.
War continued. David went out and fought Philistines, struck them with great blow, and they fled from before him.
A harmful spirit from YHWH came upon Saul while he sat in his house with spear in his hand, and David was playing with hand.
Saul sought to strike David into wall with spear, but he slipped away from before Saul. Spear struck wall, and David fled and escaped that night.
Saul sent messengers to David's house to watch him and put him to death in morning. Michal his wife told David, saying, If you do not rescue your life tonight, tomorrow you will be put to death.
Michal lowered David through window. He went, fled, and escaped.
Michal took household image, placed it in bed, put woven goat-hair at its head, and covered it with garment.
Saul sent messengers to take David, and she said, He is sick.
Saul sent messengers to see David, saying, Bring him up to me in bed to put him to death.
Messengers came, and look, household image was in bed, with woven goat-hair at its head.
Saul said to Michal, Why have you deceived me this way and sent away my enemy so he escaped? Michal said to Saul, He said to me, Send me away. Why should I put you to death?
David fled and escaped and came to Samuel at Ramah. He told him all Saul had done to him. He and Samuel went and stayed at Naioth.
It was told to Saul, saying, Look, David is at Naioth in Ramah.
Saul sent messengers to take David. They saw company of prophets prophesying, with Samuel standing as overseer upon them. Spirit of Divine came upon messengers of Saul, and they also prophesied.
They told Saul, and he sent other messengers, and they also prophesied. Saul again sent messengers, third time, and they also prophesied.
Then he himself went to Ramah and came to great cistern at Secu. He asked and said, Where are Samuel and David? One said, Look, at Naioth in Ramah.
He went there to Naioth in Ramah. Spirit of Divine came upon him also, and he went on prophesying until he came to Naioth in Ramah.
He too stripped off his garments, and he too prophesied before Samuel. He fell exposed all that day and all that night. Therefore they say, Is Saul also among prophets?

translation_notes: Jonathan opposes his father's plan and advocates for David. Family loyalty does not require protecting an abusive ruler or concealing threats.
Saul's oath not to kill David is broken almost immediately. Promises from an abuser do not by themselves establish safety or require a survivor to return.
The harmful-spirit language cannot diagnose mental illness or excuse Saul's repeated attempted murder.
David's flight and Michal's escape plan are responses to lethal domestic and state violence. Leaving, hiding, deception, and seeking sanctuary can be legitimate survival actions.
Michal acts decisively and risks royal retaliation. She is not merely David's helper, and her later explanation to Saul occurs under coercive danger.
The household image is reported without turning Michal into a symbol of religious corruption or authorizing searches, confiscation, or violence against private religious objects.
Saul uses messengers and surveillance against a household. Political authority does not sanctify stalking, siege, or extrajudicial killing.
Prophetic experience interrupts the arrest parties, but altered states, disrobing, and collapse must not be mocked, sexualized, or treated as evidence of mental illness.
Saul's exposure is a reversal of royal control, not permission to humiliate naked, distressed, or incapacitated people.
Samuel's community functions as sanctuary in the story; religious communities must not shelter abusers or force fugitives into unsafe disclosure.
safety_note: Do not romanticize Saul's spear attacks, stalking, broken oath, surveillance, or murder orders. Jonathan and Michal model resistance to abusive power, and David's escape is not cowardice. Harmful-spirit and prophetic language must never stigmatize mental illness, trauma, neurodivergence, altered states, nudity, or distress. This chapter does not authorize humiliating an exposed person or treating spiritual communities as beyond safeguarding.
